topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The office felt unnervingly quiet. Sunlight, usually a vibrant presence across the mahogany desk, seemed muted, filtered through a sudden, heavy stillness. I walked toward the door, my footsteps echoing too loudly on the polished floor. Empty. The usual scent of old paper and strong coffee was absent, replaced by a sterile neutrality that prickled at the back of my neck. I checked the main reception area; nothing. I called his direct line. Straight to voicemail. A knot tightened in my stomach, a cold, heavy pressure building behind my ribs. I kept replaying the last few weeks, searching for a clue, a sign that this sudden vacuum was an anomaly, a mistake. When I finally found his private study door ajar, the silence inside was profound, too deep for a simple vacation. Just a single, neatly folded note lay on the antique desk blotter. It was his handwriting, precise and familiar, but the message itself was a blank page.

[story 2]

"Professor Eldridge? Are you in there?" My voice sounded thin, swallowed by the cavernous silence of his study. I stood just outside the doorway, my hand hovering over the brass knob, my pulse thrumming a frantic rhythm against my fingertips. The air in the room felt strangely chilled, despite the warmth of the afternoon sun streaming through the tall windows. I took a hesitant step inside, my eyes sweeping across the room—the organized chaos of his bookshelves, the familiar leather armchair—searching for any sign of movement, any indication that he might simply be preoccupied. But the room was immaculate, unnervingly so. The only thing out of place was the lack of him. I scanned the desk, expecting a hurried note, a misplaced set of keys, anything to explain this abrupt departure. Nothing. Just the lingering impression of his presence, a phantom weight that made my breath catch in my throat.

[story 3]

I found the notice tucked beneath his favorite reading glasses. It wasn't a formal announcement; it was just a piece of heavy cream stationery, bearing his distinctive, looping script. The words were brief, almost dismissive: "I have stepped down." My hands trembled slightly as I unfolded the paper, the crisp texture feeling alien beneath my touch. I stared at the line, my mind refusing to process the simple statement. Where was the farewell? Where was the customary acknowledgement of years of shared work? A sudden, sharp sensation shot through my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I reread the note three times, searching for a hidden meaning, a coded message that would explain the void left behind. The silence of the empty office seemed to amplify the sudden, startling emptiness in my own chest.
